disease.name: Crigler-Najjar Syndrome
disease.note: Rare autosomal recessive disorder of bilirubin conjugation due to UGT1A1 mutations. Type I: complete absence of UGT1A1 activity, severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia in neonates, risk of kernicterus, no response to phenobarbital. Type II: partial UGT1A1 deficiency, less severe, responds to phenobarbital. Type I requires liver transplant; Type II can be managed medically.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Type I -> EMERGENCY transplant evaluation; Type II -> TREAT with phenobarbital, monitor; Acute kernicterus -> EMERGENCY exchange transfusion

historyFeatures.note: Presents in first 24-72h of life; profound and persistent
Arching of back; classic kernicterus sign
Distinguishing feature from hemolytic causes of neonatal jaundice

subtypes.name: Crigler-Najjar Syndrome Type I
subtypes.management: Aggressive phototherapy (12-16h/day). Exchange transfusion for crisis. Liver transplant is definitive and should be planned in first year of life. Phenobarbital: no response. Plasmapheresis as bridge. Gene therapy experimental.

subtypes.name: Crigler-Najjar Syndrome Type II
subtypes.management: Phenobarbital 5-10mg/kg/day PO. Phototherapy for acute rises. Most patients survive into adulthood with normal cognition. Risk of kernicterus during intercurrent illness. Transplant rarely needed.

laboratory.name: Total and direct (conjugated) bilirubin
laboratory.indication: very high unconjugated bilirubin (25-50 mg/dL in type I, 6-25 in type II); direct fraction <5%

laboratory.name: FBC with reticulocyte count and blood film
laboratory.indication: normal (no hemolysis); distinguishes from hemolytic causes

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT)
laboratory.indication: normal in Crigler-Najjar; helps exclude hepatocellular disease

laboratory.name: Phenobarbital response test
laboratory.indication: phenobarbital 5mg/kg/day x 2 weeks: type II shows >30% decrease in bilirubin; type I shows no response

laboratory.name: UGT1A1 genotyping
laboratory.indication: confirmatory; identifies specific mutations; type I: nonsense/frameshift; type II: missense

laboratory.name: Liver biopsy with UGT1A1 enzyme assay
laboratory.indication: rarely needed; type I: enzyme activity <1%; type II: <10%

laboratory.name: Coombs test (direct)
laboratory.indication: negative; to exclude hemolytic disease of the newborn

management.protocolReference: EASL Guideline: Crigler-Najjar; AASLD Practice Guidelines: Hyperbilirubinemia

pathways.criteria: UGT1A1 type I mutations, bilirubin >20, no phenobarbital response
pathways.treatment: Aggressive phototherapy (12-16h/day, special blue light 420-470nm) from birth. Exchange transfusion if bilirubin approaching exchange levels. Plasmapheresis as needed. Liver transplant is the only definitive treatment: ideally performed before 1 year of age to prevent neurologic damage. Transplant corrects bilirubin conjugation. Phototherapy as bridge to transplant. Avoid drugs that displace bilirubin from albumin (sulfonamides, ceftriaxone, aspirin). Gene therapy (AAV-mediated UGT1A1) is experimental. Monitor hearing and development.

pathways.criteria: UGT1A1 type II mutations, bilirubin <20, phenobarbital responsive
pathways.treatment: Phenobarbital 5-10mg/kg/day PO (adults: 60-120mg/day) — reduces bilirubin by >30%. Phototherapy for acute rises during illness. Avoid fasting (triggers hyperbilirubinemia). Avoid drugs that displace bilirubin. Most live normal lives; develop kernicterus only during extreme stress. No transplant needed in most. Monitor bilirubin during illness. Genetic counseling.
